dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 1 responding for unconscious/fainting at 1322 pm near-fainting at --Richmond address--.
Hi sir, I'm Tim with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I started feeling very dizzy. Later I almost fainted outside.
Are you awake and aware right now?
Can you tell me what county we're in?
This is Richmond. I'm Mary Jane Lewis.
What was the time when the dizziness began?
It started about thirty minutes ago.
Your airway's clear, can you speak with me easily?
Yes, I can. No trouble at all.
Mr. Lewis, we're going to check your breathing now.
Your breathing is 20 per minute.
Your pulse is 68 and steady.
Your blood pressure is 84 over 52.
Your oxygen saturation is at 99%.
Your blood sugar is measured at 182 mg/dL.
You didn't have any chest pain or vomiting, right?
No, but I feel weak in my general strength is what I meant when I said "weakness in arms," but I don't have current arm-specific weakness now.
Any past medical issues we need to know about?
Yes-I have a history of hypertension and recent stroke.
What medications are you currently taking?
To be honest, I can't remember the names right now. The last bag I had medication in the house, but it was recently discharged from the hospital so I don't yet have the home medications yet.
Are you allergic to any medication or substances?
No known allergies (NKA).
What is the last thing you ate or drank?
He had orange juice about an hour ago.
Can you tell me what led to the dizziness starting?
I was stepping outside to find the children when I nearly fell.
May I see your insurance card?
Yes, here it is.
On a scale of 0-10, how severe is your dizziness?
I would say a 4. It's bothersome, but not the worst.
What does the dizziness feel like?
A spinning or floating sensation more than a throbbing or sharp pain. Doesn't hurt but just somehow 'floats.'
Your skin is normal, cool, and slightly clammy.
I'm going to quickly perform a check of your chest with an ECG lead placement. Can you lie back slightly to give me room for the pads?
Sure, I can do that for a moment.
We captured 12-lead ECG findings earlier. We noted some abnormal Q waves in V3 and V4, possibly from a prior infarct.
There is some T wave abnormality in lateral leads suggestive of possible lateral ischemia. Also, we see evidence of possible left atrial enlargement in leads V1 and V2.
We also observed frequent premature ventricular contractions and other rhythm irregularities. All are already documented in our protocol notes.
Let me listen again and confirm there are no adventitious sounds or unusual breath sounds in either lung field now.
Your breathing is still clear and there are no added noises in either side. Now it is down to 18 per minute and not labored at all.
Your respiratory rate is now 16 per minute and consistently good depth-indicating sufficient effort. Still no signs of respiratory distress recorded so far.
Your latest EKG is still consistent with normal sinus rhythm and no acute changes observed at the last check before transport preparation started.
Blood pressure has improved slightly to 83 over 59 now from earlier 84/52 at 13:55.
Re-checking your glucose results show unable to obtain-a consistent finding so far. Your last non-fast value was 182 but current test results are unobtainable again now at transport start.
We are now inserting IV access in one of the veins in your arm; it will help us administer fluids during transport as part of the care protocol for hypotension.

After securing IV access, we are delivering a small fluid bolus to help improve your blood pressure slightly as seen earlier. Your systolic has increased to 109 now at last reading on route data in protocol.
Based on the findings, we will be following Protocol 3-13 for hypotension and shock per department standing orders. He has a recent history of a stroke and showed a prior clot.
We've already started 4-lead ECG monitoring following your last normal sinus rhythm. No acute new findings observed with repeat monitoring before move decisions were made.

Your BP since the last recorded is 80 over 60 during the initial transport phase-lower than observed before IV was administered, but it is natural to experience some fluctuation with stress and transport onset.
Your respiratory rate is 18 with continued normal breath pattern during transport start phase as observed just after we began helping you into initial transfer.
Has the dizziness improved for you since we started fluids and made our first situational interventions like IV and glucose monitoring as captured for first-stage medical response so far in our log?
Yes, the dizziness has improved and I am feeling a bit better now.
We are taking you to Memorial Regional Medical Center due to the need for neurological evaluation. You had recent stroke and should be monitored for underlying clot.
Please relax and lay flat back so we can help you onto the stretcher while ensuring your neck and spine are supported-no twisting or jarring as guided by your last safe transfer.
I understand and agree to follow instructions for movement to transport safety planning.
Your oxygen saturation has decreased to 97% since we moved you to the stretcher. We are monitoring closely.
Again, your current glucose is unobtainable, but the prior elevated non-fasting result measured at 182 continues to provide baseline guidance for trend tracking.
Can you move your body under our assistance now as we begin the transfer?.
Yes, with your help I start to do that now .
Here is the patient's insurance information, recent stroke history, IV placed in an extremity vein, current BP readings, and EKG documentation for the ER nurses.
Understood. He will be evaluated shortly.
This is a 72-year-old male with a reported history of prior stroke and primary hypertension.
Has there been any other changes in consciousness or physical presentation from when he was last assessed?
His GCS remains at 15, with eye=4, verbal=5, and motor=6. He is alert and oriented times 4. He has no complaints of pain reported during assessment and transport.
Thank you for the detailed report. No confusion or deficits is shown as yet during transporting phase.
His near-syncopal episode, according to report, improved during transport after receiving IV fluids and an increase in his BP based on reported methodologies for stabilization.
We've taken a note. GCS is 15 and he's alert and oriented throughout the ride to now.
All critical care steps and charting have been documented and filed accurately in transport logs as required.
All updates about interventions have been understood from your report.

The 12-lead ECG, performed and documented for this patient, indicated abnormal Q waves in V3/V4 and also findings consistent with left atrial enlargement and possible lateral ischemia-this was part of our standard assessment per protocol.
Will review with the ER and stroke team immediately. Wait-no reported pain here-confirming details once more.
His current pulse is 67, oxygen saturation at 97%, and neurological status stable. No new symptoms reported during transport.
Insurance and personal identification have been formally handed off now.
He denies experiencing any head pain or new sensations like echo-like waves on transportation, as per his own statement to us.
He was transferred to the stretcher with minimal assistance and without resistance. We noted safe handling.
Fluid administration and IV access, placed in an extremity vein, were properly initiated, documented, and coordinated with protocol execution standards.
Received all interventions. Your team followed protocol during the transfer.
His condition remains stable with no sudden fluctuations in BP or neurological response reported.
Transport was smooth with good IV maintenance, as per your team's monitoring log.
He turned to lie on his back with our help and is now in a relaxed position for emergency transport team handling.
Stable vitals and structured move. Is he able to still move his arms and legs freely despite stroke risk?
Yes, he can use all extremities without significant problems now according to our coordination check.
That's good to hear. Transfer is complete. All documentation is received and on file.
All care and treatment have been detailed in our notes for transfer readiness.
We'll be handling him from here forward. Thank you for assisting through these initial emergency stages.
Everything is covered and ready for your team to take over.
Let me know if there are any sudden concerns about his condition before transfer ends.
All current areas are clear; he is ready to be turned over promptly for in-hospital care.
We'll take it from here..
